title: Integration by parts
description: Practice finding indefinite integrals using the method of integration by parts.

hints:
We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\cos x \,dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \sin x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int x\cos x\,dx = x\sin x-\int\sin x\,dx\,$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~=x\sin x+\cos x+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=e^{5x} dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int e^{5x}dx = \dfrac15e^{5x}$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int xe^{5x}dx = x\cdot\dfrac15e^{5x}-\int\dfrac15e^{5x}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\,=\dfrac x5e^{5x}-\dfrac1{25}e^{5x}+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv=x^2\  dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1xdx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int x^2dx = \dfrac13x^3\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int x^2\ln xdx = \ln x\cdot\dfrac13x^3-\int\dfrac13x^3\cdot\dfrac1xdx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~\,=\dfrac{x^3\,\ln x }3-\int\dfrac{x^2}3dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~\,=\dfrac{x^3\,\ln x}3-\dfrac19x^3+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv= dx~\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1xdx~$ and $~v = x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \ln x\,dx = x\ln x-\int x\cdot\dfrac1x\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad=x\ln x-x+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=e^{-x} dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int e^{-x}dx = -e^{-x}\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\,\int xe^{-x}dx = x\cdot\big(-e^{-x}\big)-\int-e^{-x}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~= (-xe^{-x})-e^{-x}+C $

$\displaystyle~\qquad\qquad\qquad= -e^{-x}(x+1)+C$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\sqrt{x+2}\, dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \dfrac23(x+2)^{3/2}\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int x\sqrt{x+2}\,dx = x\cdot\dfrac23(x+2)^{3/2}-\int\dfrac23(x+2)^{3/2}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad=x\cdot\dfrac23(x+2)^{3/2}-\dfrac23\cdot\dfrac25(x+2)^{5/2}+C$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad=x\cdot\dfrac23(x+2)^{3/2}-\dfrac4{15}(x+2)^{5/2}+C\,$.
We can first rewrite this problem as $~~\displaystyle\int\ln(x^{1/2})\,dx = \dfrac12\int\ln x\,dx\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv= dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1x\,dx~$ and $~v = x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\dfrac12\int \ln x\,dx = \dfrac12\Big(x\ln x-\int x\cdot\dfrac1x\,dx\Big)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=\dfrac12x\ln x-\dfrac12x+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv= \dfrac1{x^2}\,dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1xdx~$ and $~v = -\dfrac1x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \dfrac{\ln x}{x^2}dx =-\dfrac{\ln x}x+\int\dfrac1{x^2}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\,=-\dfrac{\ln x}x-\dfrac1x+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\cos(\pi x) \,dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $\displaystyle ~v =\int\cos(\pi x)\,dx=  \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}\pi\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle~\qquad\int x\cos(x)\ dx = \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}-\int\dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\,=  \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}-\frac1{\pi}\Big(-\dfrac{\cos(\pi x)}{\pi}\Big)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad=  \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}+\dfrac{\cos(\pi x)}{\pi^2}+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \tan^{-1}x~$ and $~dv=dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac{1}{x^2+1}~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int dx = x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \tan^{-1}x\,dx = x\tan^{-1}x-\int\dfrac{x}{x^2+1}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~\,=x\tan^{-1}x-\dfrac12\ln(x^2+1)+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \tan^{-1}(2x)~$ and $~dv=dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac{2}{4x^2+1}\,dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int dx = x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \tan^{-1}(2x)\,dx = x\tan^{-1}(2x)-\int\dfrac{2x}{4x^2+1}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~\,=x\tan^{-1}(2x)-\dfrac14\ln(4x^2+1)+C\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \sin^{-1}x$ and $~dv=dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\, dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int dx = x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \sin^{-1}x\,dx = x\sin^{-1}x-\int\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$

We can determine this second integral using a $~u$-substitution. Let $~u=1-x^2$.

Then $~du=-2x\,dx\,$, or $~-\dfrac12du=x\, dx\,$.

Now we have $~~\displaystyle\int\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx=\int-\dfrac12\cdot\dfrac1{\sqrt{u}}\, du=-\sqrt{u}+C\,$.

Our final result is now

$\displaystyle\qquad\int \sin^{-1}x\,dx = x\sin^{-1}x-(-\sqrt{u}+C) =x\sin^{-1}x+\sqrt{1-x^2}+C\,$.
